coredata.dc:title: SEM and TEM characterization of sodium alanate doped with TiCl3 or small Ti clusters (Ti13·6THF)
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2005 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Léon, Aline
Kircher, Oliver
Rösner, Harald
Décamps, Brigitte
Leroy, Eric
Fichtner, Maximilian
Percheron-Guégan, Annick
coredata.dc:description: The evolution of the microstructure under desorption and absorption of hydrogen in sodium alanate doped with 5mol.% Ti on the basis of either TiCl3 or Ti13·6THF is presented. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) have been applied to investigate ball-milled samples during and after various hydrogen desorption and absorption cycles. Upon the phase transitions the change in the microstructure is characterized by an increase in the porosity. The overall transformation is a random and irreversible process. After several cycles in the dehydrogenated state Al crystallites of 300nm in size have been detected. Additionally, EDX analysis suggests a homogeneous distribution of Ti together with Na, Al and Cl. Moreover, at different stages of the reaction a pronounced variation of the Al concentration in the particle has been observed in contrast to all other elements. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) combined with EDX analysis does not detect the presence of Ti particles implying that the particles size must be less than 0.8nm.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrogen storage materials
Sodium alanate
Ti-based precursor
Scanning and transmission electron microscopy
Nanostructures

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Microstructure of NaAlH4 doped with TiCl3
Microstructure of NaAlH4 doped with Ti cluster

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Low magnification scanning electron micrograph (SEM) of NaAlH4 mixed with 5mol.% of TiCl3 in a mortar. (b) Enlarged micrograph of a region from (a).
(a) Low magnification scanning electron micrograph (SEM) of NaAlH4 ball-milled with 5mol.% of TiCl3. (b) Enlarged micrograph of a region from (a) showing more details.
TEM image of a grid revealing the shape and size distribution of the particles.

STEM micrographs and corresponding elemental-distribution map of Al, Na, Cl and Ti of NaAlH4 doped with 5mol.% of TiCl3 samples after ball milling (bm), during the first desorption (dd) and during the subsequent absorption (da).

Bright-field transmission electron micrograph (TEM) and the corresponding dark-field scanning transmission electron micrograph (STEM) of NaAlH4 doped with 5mol.% of TiCl3 samples after ball milling (bm), after the first desorption (ad), after subsequent absorption (aa), after the eighth absorption (a8a) and after the ninth desorption (a9d).

High-resolution transmission electron micrograph (HRTEM) of NaAlH4 doped with 5mol.% of TiCl3 of samples corresponding to those of Fig. 5. The inter-planar distance is obtained by averaging the distance between 10 lattice fringes.
Scanning electron micrograph (SEM) of Ti13·6THF as synthesized.
(a) Low magnification scanning electron micrograph of NaAlH4 doped with 5mol.% of Ti13·6THF of samples (bm), (dd) and (da). (b) Enlarged micrograph of a region from (a) demonstrating details.

Scanning transmission electron micrograph and the corresponding elemental-distribution map of Al, Na and Ti in NaAlH4 doped with 5mol.% of Ti13·6THF of samples after ball milling (bm), during desorption (dd) and during absorption (da).

Bright-field transmission electron micrograph (TEM) and the corresponding dark-field scanning transmission electron micrograph (STEM) of NaAlH4 doped with 5mol.% of Ti13·6THF after ball milling (bm), after one absorption (aa), after 2 cycles (a2d), after the eighth absorption (a8a) and after the ninth desorption (a9d).

High-resolution transmission electron micrograph (HRTEM) of NaAlH4 doped with 5mol.% of Ti13·6THF samples corresponding to those of Fig. 10. The inter-planar distance is obtained by averaging the distance between 10 lattices fringes.

article.ce:title: SEM and TEM characterization of sodium alanate doped with TiCl3 or small Ti clusters (Ti13·6THF)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institut für Nanotechnologie, Forschungszentrum Karlsruhe in der Helmholtz-Gemeinschaft, P.O. Box 3640, 76021 Karlsruhe, Germany

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratoire de Chimie Métallurgique des Terres Rares, Institut Fédératif des Sciences Chimiques Seine/Amont, 2-8 Rue Henri Dunant, 94320 Thiais cedex, France

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The evolution of the microstructure under desorption and absorption of hydrogen in sodium alanate doped with 5mol.% Ti on the basis of either TiCl3 or Ti13·6THF is presented. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) have been applied to investigate ball-milled samples during and after various hydrogen desorption and absorption cycles. Upon the phase transitions the change in the microstructure is characterized by an increase in the porosity. The overall transformation is a random and irreversible process. After several cycles in the dehydrogenated state Al crystallites of 300nm in size have been detected. Additionally, EDX analysis suggests a homogeneous distribution of Ti together with Na, Al and Cl. Moreover, at different stages of the reaction a pronounced variation of the Al concentration in the particle has been observed in contrast to all other elements. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) combined with EDX analysis does not detect the presence of Ti particles implying that the particles size must be less than 0.8nm.

ce:section.ce:para:
Hydrogen stored in the form of hydrides avoids many of the problems that are encountered with compressed or liquefied hydrogen [1]. However, the use of such a storage system will depend on identifying a compound with a sufficient absorption capacity as well as thermodynamic and kinetic properties, which allows operation at moderate temperatures and pressures. So far, complex metal hydrides, in particular sodium alanate (NaAlH4) doped with Ti-based materials, as a medium temperature reversible hydride (Eqs. (1) and (2)) satisfy several of the prerequisites for fuel cell applications [2].
(1) 3NaAlH4 ⇆Na3AlH6 +2Al+3H2 (3.7wt.%ofH2)
(2)
N
a 3
Al
H 6
⇄ 3 NaH + Al +
3 2
H 2
( 1.9
wt . %
of
H 2
)
However, the mechanism involved during the absorption and desorption reaction of hydrogen as well as the role of titanium to ease the thermal activation process is not yet understood. Recent X-ray absorption (XAS) and X-ray photoelectron (XPS) investigations have shown that in the case of TiCl3 the Ti is completely reduced to the metallic state [3–5] after 30min of ball milling [6]. The information obtained by XAS concerning the chemical state of titanium can be complemented by analysis performed on the atomic length scale using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Analytical TEM allows the simultaneous examination of micro-structural features through high-resolution imaging and the acquisition of chemical and crystallographic information from small particles. Moreover, this technique provides information on the evolution of their morphology. Hence, information can be obtained concerning the role of titanium and the mechanism of the desorption and absorption reaction in sodium alanate.

In previous studies, SEM and TEM investigations were performed on pure sodium alanate as well as on sodium alanate doped with different precursors, such as titanium tetrabutylate, Ti(OBu)4 [4,7]; titanium tetrabutylate in combination with zirconium tetrapropylate, Ti(OBu)4/Zr(OPr)4 [8]; titanium chloride, TiCl3 [4,9]; Ti cluster (Ti13·6THF) which consists of a core of 13 Ti atoms coordinated by O atoms from tetrahydrofuran (THF) [4,9] and TiF3 [10].

It was shown that the solvent used in the purification of sodium alanate has an influence on the final particle sizes and shapes [7]. Moreover, the investigations of NaAlH4 doped with Ti-based compounds have shown that the microstructural shape and size of the particles depend on the nature of the precursor and the doping method [8].

NaAlH4 doped with a tandem catalyst (2mol.% Ti(OBu)4 and 2mol.% Zr(OPr)4) by high-energy ball milling (Spex mill, milling time 2min or 3h) have been investigated by SEM [8]. After ball milling for 2min, the well-defined angular features of the individual particles were smoother compared to the initial state of the sample. Additionally, there were indications that the particles have agglomerated during the doping/milling procedure [8]. Absorption and desorption cycles of hydrogen significantly altered the morphology of the particles but particle break-up or fragmentation has not been observed even after 5 cycles in contrast to intermetallic hydrides [8]. Furthermore, decomposition between Al and Na was observed and a correlation between the Ti catalyst and Al-rich surfaces was noticed after only 5 cycles.

NaAlH4 particles doped with 4mol.% Ti(OBu)4 by a wet doping process [4,7] investigated by SEM after the 17th dehydrogenation were highly porous and composed of particles of less than 5μm in size. Elemental mapping of Na and Al suggested that the doped material in the dehydrogenated state was separated into NaH and Al phases [7]. Furthermore, TEM micrographs obtained on particles after the first desorption of hydrogen showed a crystalline Al phase (size of about 10nm–1μm) [4]. EDX analyses indicated that titanium was present only in the Al phase.

NaAlH4 doped with Ti13·6THF [4] (2mol.% Ti by ball milling 3h in a stainless steel vial) was investigated in the first dehydrogenated state by TEM. Particles had a globular shape and were occasionally fused with each other. Ti13·6THF doped alanate was characterized by a lower degree of crystallinity in contrast to Ti(OBu)4 doped samples. EDX analyses also confirmed the disperse nature of this material and showed a homogeneous distribution of Ti in the Al phase. Similar results were obtained with dehydrogenated TiCl3 doped NaAlH4 [4] (2mol.% Ti by ball milling 3h in a stainless steel vial using a Spex ball mill). In that case, the distribution of Ti in the Al phase was found to be less uniform in comparison to the Ti colloid as a dopant.

Recently, a combined TEM and SEM investigation of NaAlH4 doped with TiF3 [10] (2mol.% Ti by ball milling 30min, 350rpm in a Fletscher 7 planetary mill) suggested that after ball milling no reduction of TiF3 occurred, leading to a low degree of mixing between the TiF3 and the alanate phase. In contrast, after 15 cycles a decomposition of TiF3 is observed as well as a higher degree of mixing between the two phases.

As described above, previous microstructural investigations were carried out using a liquid precursor [4,7,8], the Ti colloid, TiCl3 [4,9] and TiF3 [10]. Moreover, all the samples were studied only in the dehydrogenated state. So far to our knowledge, no SEM or TEM work has been published concerning the hydrogenated state of doped sodium alanate. Additionally, questions still remain concerning the distribution of Ti in the particles in the dehydrogenated state as well as in the hydrogenated case. It is an open question, for example, whether the homogeneous distribution of Ti in the Al phase observed in the decomposed state [4] is preserved at different stages of the dehydrogenation/rehydrogenation reaction as well as in the hydrogenated state.

Herein, we consider the TiCl3 and the Ti13·6THF doped sodium alanate as these two precursors were shown to be more efficient than other dopants investigated so far [11–14]. Thus, we report on the evolution of the microstructure at different stages of the desorption and absorption reaction of hydrogen in NaAlH4 doped with Ti-based precursors by ball milling. In this study, a concentration of 5mol.% Ti on the basis of Ti13·6THF has been used in contrast to earlier studies (0.9mol.% [11], 1.8mol.% [11,12] or 2mol.% [4,13]) because X-ray absorption spectroscopy [3,15], X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy [6] and kinetic measurements [16] have been carried out with NaAlH4 doped with 5mol.% Ti on the basis of Ti13·6THF or TiCl3 by ball milling (Fritsch P6 planetary mixer/mill, silicon nitride vial and balls, milling time 30min). The conditions described above have been selected in order to ease comparison and to complement results between these techniques. As it was mentioned that a correlation between elements would appear after only 5 cycles [8], we investigated sodium alanate doped with 5mol.% Ti on the basis of Ti13·6THF or TiCl3 during and after the first desorption of hydrogen, during and after the first absorption of hydrogen, after 8 hydrogenation cycles and in the following decomposed state. All the samples have been analyzed using SEM, TEM and HRTEM. Finally, an attempt to understand the role of titanium and how the bulk transformation is taking place is presented.

All sample preparations were done in an argon-filled glove box equipped with a recirculation system to keep the water and oxygen concentrations below 1ppm. Chemical operations were performed on the bench under purified nitrogen using Schlenk tube techniques.

Commercially available NaAlH4 (Chemetall, Frankfurt) was purified by a Soxhlet extraction with THF as described elsewhere [11]. The samples doped with a Ti-based catalyst were prepared using either TiCl3 (99.999%, Sigma–Aldrich) or Ti13·6THF [11–13].

In an argon glove box, a 80ml silicon nitride vial containing balls made of the same material was filled with either 285mg of TiCl3 or 513mg of Ti13·6THF (purity of 32%) and 2g of purified sodium alanate and sealed. The ball to powder weight ratio was about 20:1 and the milling was carried out in a Fritsch P6 planetary mixer/mill for 30min at 600rpm.

Absorption and desorption of hydrogen were carried out in a modified Sievert-type apparatus. A more detailed description of the apparatus and the reactor used can be found elsewhere [11,12]. Micro-structural characterization was done on samples quenched at a defined degree of the dehydrogenation–rehydrogenation reaction (cf. Table 1 ).

Samples were prepared in an argon-filled glove box by spreading the powders on a carbon film for SEM analysis and on a holey carbon film supported on a copper grid for TEM investigations. For HRTEM analysis, the powders were dispersed in toluene and one drop of the supernatant liquid was placed on a holey carbon film of a Cu-grid. Once the toluene evaporated, it left a distribution of particles on the support film. All the samples were prepared and transferred to the microscopes under an Ar atmosphere. For SEM (Gemini Leo 1530) and TEM (JEOL 2000 FX) investigations, samples were exposed for about 1s to air just during the connection of the sample holder to the microscope whereas for TEM (Philips Tecnai F20 ST) analysis with high-resolution they were exposed for about 1min due to the mounting of the grid on the sample holder and the subsequent connection to the microscope.

Samples were first examined in a scanning electron microscope operating at 20kV equipped with an X-ray energy dispersive spectrometer for chemical analysis. Then, they were investigated in greater detail with a JEOL 2000 FX (tungsten filament) and a Philips Tecnai F20 ST transmission electron microscope (Field-emission gun operated at 4.1keV extraction voltage, super-twin lens, C s =1.2mm, point resolution of 0.235nm at Scherzer focus, info limit of 0.14nm) operating at 200kV. Both microscopes were equipped with a Si(Li) X-ray energy-dispersive spectrometer (XEDS from Oxford Instruments ISIS 200 and EDAX, respectively) to perform elemental-distribution mapping, point and line-scan analysis. TEM and STEM bright-field images were collected with the JEOL 2000 FX. Images in the STEM mode were collected using a high-angle annular dark-field (HAADF) detector with the Philips Tecnai F20 ST microscope. Additionally to obtain crystallographic information high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) investigations were performed with this microscope. HRTEM micrographs were analysed with the software package Digital Micrograph (Version 3.5.2, Gatan Co.) in order to perform Fast Fourier Transform.

The composition of the samples was measured first with the scanning electron microscope, which was calibrated with the following standards: YBa2Cu3O7 for oxygen, NaAlSi3O8 for Na, Al2O3 for Al, KCl for Cl, pure titanium for Ti. Elemental maps were acquired with the JEOL 2000 FX by scanning the sample in a fast mode and cumulating the counts during 100 scans in order to minimize the damages due to the beam intensity. The correlation between different elements was determined by superimposing elemental maps. Quantitative nanobeam spot, linescan or square analyses were performed with the Philips Tecnai F20 ST microscope using the Cliff–Lorimer method [17].
During the analysis, we did not face any charging problem under the beam with doped alanate, as these samples were conductive (as shown also by XPS analysis).

In order to avoid the decomposition of samples in the hydrogenated state (sodium alanate doped with TiCl3 in the ball-milled state) under the beam, special observation conditions involving STEM with fast mapping were used. Fast mapping involves the recording of images with a resolution of 512×532 pixels and a dwell time of 0.12ms/pixel. In those conditions, the morphology of the observed particles was checked to be unchanged after mapping. For the decomposition to occur (modification of the morphology), beam has to be focused in TEM mode (100nm probe) for a few minutes.
During the analysis several particles have been examined for each sample. In this paper the most significant ones are selected and presented.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microstructure of NaAlH4 doped with TiCl3
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1(a) shows an overview of NaAlH4 mixed with 5mol.% TiCl3 in a mortar. NaAlH4 crystallites, characterized by EDX analysis, present a well-defined angular shape with particle size ranging from about 40 to 350μm whereas TiCl3 particles do not reveal such a defined character. An enlargement of a region from Fig. 1(a and b) indicates that the TiCl3 has a tile like-structure. Fig. 2 shows the microstructure of the sample after ball milling at low (Fig. 2(a)) and higher magnification (Fig. 2(b)), respectively. The micrographs reveal that submicron sized particles form aggregates of several micrometers upon ball milling.

Observations reveal that some small particles (few nanometer in size) are not characterized by a porous structure and are found at all stages of the dehydrogenation/hydrogenation reaction. They seem to be uninvolved in the reaction although EDX analyses indicated the presence of Ti in these particles.
The highly porous character and the inhomogeneous thickness of the particles did not allow getting any valuable quantitative point or line-scan analysis.

TEM bright-field micrographs and the corresponding STEM dark-field images of samples (bm), (ad), (aa) (Fig. 5 ) confirm the results shown above and again reveal regions of different contrast and the porous character of the material. Qualitative EDX spot analysis performed with a nano-beam (2nm) on the (bm) sample indicated the existence of alanate phase (spot 1) and Al phase (spot 2). Qualitative line-scan analysis performed on the sample (ad) and (aa) confirmed the presence of the elements O, Na, Al, Cl, Ti and Cu (from the grid). The dot line visible in the STEM micrograph (Fig. 5, STEM (ad) and (aa)) is the individual spot contamination during EDX line-scan measurement. From the analysis, it can be concluded that the distribution of these elements as well as the thickness along the line is random and inhomogeneous in each particle.

STEM images of samples after the eighth absorption, (a8a) and after the ninth desorption, (a9d) indicate that the shape and the microstructure of these particles are different compared to the (bm), (ad) and (aa) samples. After several cycles, in the dehydrogenated as well as in the hydrogenated state, the particle's edges are not well defined and the porosity is even more pronounced. In the hydrogenated state, semi-quantitative analysis performed on the region indicated in the micrograph (Fig. 5, STEM (a8a)) reveals the presence of Ti (1.2at.%), Cl (0.3at.%), Na (11.7at.%), Al (18.4at.%) and O (68.4at.%). In the analysis, carbon was excluded because of the carbon film on the copper grid. In the followed decomposed state, semi-quantitative EDX analysis performed on the region (Fig. 5, STEM (a9d)_1_EDX_square 1) indicated the presence of Ti (5.4at.%), Cl (2.2at.%), Na (11.1at.%), Al (17.6at.%) and O (63.7at.%). Moreover, crystallites of few nanometers with a spherical shape appeared to be embedded in the particle. Semi-quantitative EDX analysis (Fig. 5, STEM (a9d)_1_EDX_square2) performed on this region indicated the presence of an Al phase which is free of titanium. Analyses performed at higher magnification with a spot of 2nm (Fig. 5, STEM (a9d)_2) confirmed the later result. No trace of chlorine or titanium was found. After 9 cycles, in the dehydrogenated state, an Al crystallite (size of around 280nm) was detected inside the analyzed particle.

HRTEM (Fig. 6 ) performed on these samples showed fringe contrast in the crystalline parts. After 8 cycles, in sample (a8a), the measured inter-planar spacing was 0.238±0.007 and 0.206±0.006nm. Taking account the uncertainties in the measurement and using powder X-ray diffraction data [18] the above distances can correspond to reflections from several compounds. Indeed for the distance 0.238±0.007nm reflections from NaAlH4 (202), Na3AlH6 (211), NaH (200), Al (111), Al2O3 (110) or Ti (002) can be attributed. However, the state of the sample and EDX analysis make it unlikely that those reflections belong to NaH (200) or Ti (002). At this point, one reflection cannot be selected undoubtedly for the above distance. In the same way, the d-spacing 0.206±0.006nm corresponds likely to Al (200) or Al2O3 (113). In the following desorbed state, reflections from Al (200) or Al2O3 (113) can be indexed to the measured inter-planar spacing of 0.211±0.006nm, Fig. 6 (HRTEM_(a9d)_1 and HRTEM_(a9d)_2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microstructure of NaAlH4 doped with Ti cluster
ce:section.ce:para: STEM micrographs of samples (bm), (dd) and (da) as well as the elemental maps (Fig. 9 ) reveal again the highly porous character of this material. In contrast to the TiCl3 doped alanate, numerous pores are embedded in the matrix already in the ball-milled state. They are randomly distributed inside the particle and their size range is from 50nm to 3μm. Elemental distribution maps in the ball-milled state were performed in one selected region of the particle as in contrast to samples (dd) or (da) the outer part of some pores in that case was not completely free of material. At this magnification, maps reveal a homogeneous distribution of the three elements apart from two weak spots missing in the Na distribution, which are present in the Ti and Al distributions. Maps obtained from samples (dd) and (da) (even though the intensity of Ti signal is low) reveal a homogeneous distribution of the three elements and any lack of correlation between the elements could not be established.

Fig. 10 shows the TEM bright-field micrographs and the corresponding STEM dark-field images of samples (bm), (aa), after the second desorption (a2d), after the eighth absorption (a8a) and after the ninth desorption (a9d). In the first cycle, particles have a spherical shape and exhibit regions of different contrast. A clear difference appears in the shape and the microstructure after the second and the eighth cycle. Semi-quantitative EDX spot analyses with a nano-beam of 2nm sizes were performed on the (bm) sample. Peaks from C, O were identified as contamination and Cu from the grid. Concerning the other elements, equivalent distribution of Na and Al (spot 1), coexistence of Al and Ti phase (spot 2) and pure Al phase (spot 3) were identified in the ball-milled state.

Semi-quantitative analysis performed on sample (a8a) (Fig. 10, STEM_(a8a)_1) indicated the presence of Na, Al, Ti, O and C. Analysis performed at higher magnification (Fig. 10, STEM_(a8a)_2) in the bright region (square1) gave the following atomic concentrations, Ti (0.9at.%), Al (57.6at.%), Na (5.9at.%) and O (35.6at.%) from spectra (STEM_a8a_2_EDX_square 1). In contrast, in the dark region (square 2) the distribution was Ti (0.5at.%), Al (24.1at.%), Na (6.7at.%) and O (68.7at.%) from spectra (STEM_a8a_2_EDX_square 2). Thus, titanium was present everywhere. Micrographs from sample (a9d) (Fig. 10, STEM_(a9d)_1) exhibit regions with pronounced contrast. Analysis performed on the bright region (square 1) revealed the presence of Al, Na, Ti, O and C from spectrum (STEM_a9d_1_EDX_square 1) whereas the dark region (square 2) was free of titanium. Indeed, only Al, Na, O and C were detected from spectra (STEM_a9d_1_EDX_square 2). A similar result (absence of Ti) was obtained at higher magnification (Fig. 10, STEM_(a9d)_2) of the region indicated in Fig. 10, STEM_(a9d)_1.

HRTEM performed on these samples as shown in Fig. 11 display fringe contrast in the crystalline parts. In the sample (a2d), the measured lattice spacing was 0.233±0.007nm which can corresponds to several reflections. However, in this case the corresponding diffraction pattern which is shown as an inset of HRTEM_a2d_1 indicates the 〈110〉 zone axis of a cubic crystal and allows eliminating all the reflections expect the Al (111). Another measured lattice spacing in the (a2d) sample was 0.186±0.006nm which corresponds to Na3AlH6 (221) reflections. The presence of a pore embedded in the crystalline part is visible in the micrograph HRTEM_a2d_2. The lattice spacing of 0.247±0.007nm measured in sample (a8a) corresponds likely to the reflections from Na3AlH6 (221) or Al2O3 (104).

The present study shows that the morphology of the particles in NaAlH4 doped with a Ti-based compound by ball milling depends on the precursor. The main difference in the microstructure between the two precursors investigated is that the decomposition occurs already after ball milling with the Ti cluster doped sample. This result confirms similar observations made by NMR study in those samples [19].

Moreover, this study highlights the disordered character of the material. The shape of the particles is evolving with the cycles from a spherical shape to a non-spherical one. The free volume in each particle, with the evolution of hydrogen is directly visible and enhanced with the number of cycles. Additionally, the chemical composition and the boundary regions are randomly distributed at the different stages of the reaction.

Some particles with a small size do not react during desorption and absorption of hydrogen even though the presence of titanium. The percentage of particles, which is not reacting, is unknown but this leads to some extent to the fact that the theoretical capacity of 5.6wt.% has not been reached experimentally with sodium alanate doped with Ti-based materials.

Once the hydrogen is released the microstructure is significantly changed by the formation of pores. The pores are randomly distributed inside the particle and defined by various shapes and sizes. They are created by the decomposition of the alanate which starts during ball milling and with further desorption reaction more material is released from the outer region of the pores. During the transformation the change in the microstructure is a random and irreversible process. Moreover, after several cycles an Al crystallite of about 300nm in size has been identified in the dehydrogenated state of sodium alanate doped with TiCl3 confirming that relatively large crystallites of Al are formed during decomposition reactions as published in previous in situ X-ray studies [20]. As reported previously [7], the overall reversible transformation is taking place in each particle independently and without any breaking of the particle, although after 8 cycles particle's edges are not well defined.

Depending on the stage of the reaction, EDX spot analyses revealed always Al alone, together with Ti, together with Na or together with Na and Ti. Moreover, the Na is never present alone or exclusively with Ti. In samples doped with TiCl3, Cl has been identified also together with Ti, Al and Na. From our measurements after the first or the ninth cycle we cannot state that Al is always associated with Ti in the dehydrogenated state as in many cases the Al phase was found without any detectable amount of Ti.

Ti reflections were absent in the X-ray diffraction pattern recorded from the sodium alanate doped with Ti-based precursors [20,21] suggesting that the Ti phase is present in small amounts with small particle size. If metal atoms are arranged in a proper way, with conventional TEM and HRTEM metal particles of 1.5–2.0 and 0.8nm in diameter can be detected, respectively. In a previous TEM study as well as in the present work Ti or Ti/Al alloys particles were not visible indicating that the particle size was less than 1.5–2.0nm. In this work, with HRTEM crystalline parts have been observed at the different stage of dehydrogenation and hydrogenation reaction. In the case of TiCl3 or Ti cluster doped sample, EDX maps as well as line-scan analyses assure the presence of Ti in the hydride particles. However, STEM and EDX spot analysis revealed only Al particles and never Ti particles implying that the size of Ti particles is less than 0.8nm and then invisible to the present technique.

The microstructure of sodium alanate doped with 5mol.% Ti on the basis of either TiCl3 or Ti13·6THF has been investigated using SEM, TEM and HRTEM. Micrographs reveal that submicron sized particles form aggregates of several micrometers upon ball milling. Particles are characterized by the presence of numerous pores and elemental maps performed on these particles exhibit a homogeneous distribution of Al, Na, Cl and Ti. After 9 cycles, in the dehydrogenated state, Al crystallites (size of around 280nm) were detected inside the analyzed particles. After the first or the ninth cycle, EDX analysis does not reveal that Ti is exclusively associated with the Al phase in the dehydrogenated nor in the hydrogenated state of NaAlH4 doped with TiCl3 or Ti13·6THF. In contrast, elemental maps reveal a homogeneous distribution of Ti with all the other elements in both TiCl3 and Ti13·6THF doped alanate. HRTEM combined with EDX analysis does not detect Ti particles implying that the particle size must be less than 0.8nm.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Claudia Didschies and Christian Lemier are gratefully acknowledged for their help with SEM micrographs as well as Valérie Lalanne for the TEM sample preparation.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: A. Léon, O. Kircher, H. Rösner, M. Fichtner, O. Fuhr, Gordon Research Conference on Hydrogen–Metal Systems, 2003.
A. Léon, O. Kircher, J. Rothe, M. Fichtner, J. Metastable Nanocryst. Mater., in press.
Powder diffraction files, International center for diffraction data, Inorganic phases, set 1-51, USA, 2001.
